records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.child_node: Does the facility use barriers to mitigate a high-speed avenue of approach?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not use barriers to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach. Barriers would reduce vehicle speeds and/or prevent vehicle penetration.
records.ofc_text: Evaluate vehicle traffic patterns near the facility. Design and implement strategies to reduce vehicle speeds, improve pedestrian safety, and reduce the threat of vehicle approach velocities.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), section 3.5, p. 3-37, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Install barriers to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach, deny vehicle entry, and provide perimeter protection. Options include, but are not limited to, fixed and retractable bollards, engineered planters, heavy objects and trees, walls and ha-ha barriers, water obstacles, and Jersey barriers.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings, (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Install fixed bollards, engineered planters, and/or heavy objects to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Install a bollard system that is well-integrated into the perimeter security design and the streetscape to minimize visual impact. Bollard height should typically not be more than 30 inches. Bollard spacing should be between 36 and 43 inches, depending on the kind of traffic expected and the needs of pedestrians and the handicapped.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Install planters with considerable reinforcing and below-grade depth for effectiveness. Maintain a maximum distance of 4 feet between planters and other permanent streetscape elements, depending on the kind of traffic anticipated. Orient planters in a direction parallel to the curb or primary flow of pedestrian traffic. Do not place planter or a line of planters perpendicular to the curb.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Install heavy objects that can resist vehicle penetration, such as boulders, sculptures, and trees. Such objects need varying degrees of embedment and reinforcement, depending on their weight, footprint, and height/width ratio.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Explore the possibility of erecting walls, which includes retaining walls and freestanding walls, that may be constructed of concrete, concrete masonry, brick, natural stone, or other materials typically reinforced with steel.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Install Jersey barriers. To be effective, Jersey barriers need to be embedded and include vertical anchorage of steel reinforcing through the barrier into the pavement.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Install Jersey barriers as a temporary solution. Jersey barriers are relatively inexpensive and readily available.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Explore the possibility of adding speed controls (e.g., serpentines or speed bumps) to limit vehicle speed at impact.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-5, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Explore the possibility of installing active barriers, such as retractable bollards or a wedge system, to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach. Active barriers must be tended at all times by trained personnel and require regular maintenance to ensure continued operation.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-56 to 2-61, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06 pdf for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-1 to A-6, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat situations).
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Use vehicles as temporary physical barriers to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility during elevated threat conditions.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.vulnerability: The facility uses barriers to mitigate some, but not all, high-speed avenues of approach. Barriers would reduce vehicle speeds and/or prevent vehicle penetration.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #2) Install barriers to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach, deny vehicle entry, and provide perimeter protection. Options include, but are not limited to, fixed and retractable bollards, engineered planters, heavy objects and trees, walls and ha-ha barriers, water obstacles, and Jersey barriers.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #3) Install fixed bollards, engineered planters, and/or heavy objects to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #4) Install a bollard system that is well-integrated into the perimeter security design and the streetscape to minimize visual impact. Bollard height should typically not be more than 30 inches. Bollard spacing should be between 36 and 43 inches, depending on the kind of traffic expected and the needs of pedestrians and the handicapped.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #5) Install well-designed planters with considerable reinforcing and below-grade depth for effectiveness. Maintain a maximum distance of 4 feet between planters and other permanent streetscape elements, depending on the kind of traffic anticipated. Orient planters in a direction parallel to the curb or primary flow of pedestrian traffic. Do not place planter or a line of planters perpendicular to the curb.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #6) Install heavy objects that can resist vehicle penetration, such as boulders, sculptures, and trees. Such objects need varying degrees of embedment and reinforcement, depending on their weight, footprint, and height/width ratio.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #7) Explore the possibility of erecting walls, which includes retaining walls and freestanding walls, that may be constructed of concrete, concrete masonry, brick, natural stone, or other materials typically reinforced with steel.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #8) Install Jersey barriers. To be effective, Jersey barriers need to be embedded and include vertical anchorage of steel reinforcing through the barrier into the pavement.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #9) Install Jersey barriers as a temporary solution. Jersey barriers are relatively inexpensive and readily available.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #10) Explore the possibility of adding speed controls (e.g., serpentines or speed bumps) to limit vehicle speed at impact.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-5, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #11) Explore the possibility of installing active barriers, such as retractable bollards or a wedge system, to mitigate high-speed avenues of approach. Active barriers must be tended at all times by trained personnel and require regular maintenance to ensure continued operation.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-56 to 2-61, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #12) Explore the possibility of using CPTED principles, concepts, and strategies (e.g., water barriers, landscaping) to install barriers. Consult Appendix A of Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, and Tables 2.4 and 2.5 of Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), available at http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips- 06 pdf for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014; FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security, Guidance Against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), Appendix A, p. A-1 to A-6, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.-

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #13) Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat situations).
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #14) Use vehicles as temporary physical barriers to eliminate straight-line vehicular access to the facility during elevated threat conditions.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have K-rated barriers. K-rated barriers can significantly reduce the risk of vehicle penetration.
records.ofc_text: Install K-rated barriers in areas vulnerable to invasive vehicles. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Facilities Physical Security Measures Guideline (ASIS GDL FPSM-2009), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, section 3.2.1.2., p. 13; FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.1.1, p. 2-14, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of upgrading all barriers to meet K-rated standards. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Facilities Physical Security Measures Guideline (ASIS GDL FPSM-2009), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, section 3.2.1.2., p. 13; FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.1.1, p. 2-14, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a high-speed avenue(s) of approach?
records.vulnerability: Although the facility has K-rated barriers in some areas, it lacks K-rated barriers in other areas that are vulnerable to invasive vehicles. K-rated barriers can significantly reduce the risk of vehicle penetration.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #15) Install K-rated barriers in areas vulnerable to invasive vehicles. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.

records.parent_question: Does the facility use barriers to enforce standoff from the facility?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not use barriers to enforce an effective standoff distance, i.e., the distance between the facility and the potential location for an explosive detonation.
records.ofc_text: Install barriers to increase standoff distance and reduce damage from a potential explosive device. Options include, but are not limited to, fixed and retractable bollards, engineered planters, heavy objects and trees, walls and ha-ha barriers, water obstacles, and Jersey barriers.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility use barriers to enforce standoff from the facility?
records.ofc_text: Install fixed bollards, engineered planters, and/or heavy objects, such as boulders and sculptures, to increase standoff distance from a potential explosive device and reduce damage.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 2.3.4.4, p. 2-43 to 2-56, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility use barriers to enforce standoff from the facility?
records.ofc_text: Explore alternatives if it is not possible to create sufficient standoff distance through the use of barriers. Options may include hardening the building and minimizing hazardous flying debris during an explosive event (e.g., window protective measures such as anti-shatter film, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass).
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 3.3, p. 3-31 to 3-60, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility use barriers to enforce standoff from the facility?
records.ofc_text: Employ temporary passive barriers (e.g., Jersey barriers) to increase standoff distance as needed (e.g., under special circumstances or during elevated threat conditions).
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 1.8.3, p. 1-40, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility use barriers to enforce standoff from the facility?

records.vulnerability: Ground-floor windows do not have protective measures to mitigate the hazardous effects of flying glass during an explosive event.
records.ofc_text: Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Apply anti-shatter film to existing glass to provide fragment retention and reduce the overall velocity of glass fragments at failure, especially near regularly occupied and mass-gathering areas. Anti-shatter film may mitigate the impact of wind-blown debris and seismic stress in addition to explosive force by preventing pieces of broken glass from becoming lethal projectiles.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.vulnerability: Ground-floor windows do not have protective measures to deter entry. If they are not hardened, standard windows may be the weak link in a structure's barrier protection. Most standard windows can be penetrated with hand tools in less than a minute.
records.ofc_text: Install protective coverings, metal grills, and/or steel mesh to harden windows against penetration.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.3, p. 3-20 to 3-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility in a building?
records.ofc_text: Determine whether windows are required. If not, explore the feasibility of permanently changing to a solid wall with no glazing, which would enhance penetration delay and help protect personnel, property, and operations from blast effects.

records.vulnerability: Ground-floor windows have limited protective measures (e.g., thermally-tempered glass). During a blast event, thermally-tempered glass initially may be propelled into cohesive clumps that only fragment into smaller pieces upon impact; in addition, the high velocity of the fragments constitutes a severe hazard.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #1) Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. records.ofc_text: Replace glass doors with solid doors without glass panels, if possible.

records.vulnerability: Glass doors at the facility lack protective measures to minimize fragmentation after an explosive event. Following such incidents, a high number of injuries can result from flying glass fragments.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #1) Conduct an assessment to determine additional appropriate, feasible mitigation measures to reduce the vulnerability and risk associated with flying glass during an explosive event. Options to consider include, but are not limited to, anti-shatter film, blast curtains, bullet-resistant glass, and laminated glass.

records.vulnerability: Facility doors with exposed hinges/pins are at risk of tampering. Exterior door hinges may be removed to open a locked door to gain unauthorized access.
records.ofc_text: Relocate hinges on the interior, if possible, or provide concealed hinges to reduce the likelihood of tampering. Hinge pins could also be welded to hinge to preclude easy removal.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.4, p. 3-31, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility in a building?
records.ofc_text: Secure hinges, hinge pins, and hasps against unauthorized removal by using spot welds or peened mounting bolts.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.4, p. 3-31, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility in a building?
records.vulnerability: Doors at the facility do not have reinforced strike plates. Reinforced strike plates harden doors against break-ins.
records.ofc_text: Install reinforced strike plates on facility doors to deter unauthorized forced entry.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.3.4, p. 3-31, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.vulnerability: Outside air intakes are accessible and susceptible to the introduction of CBR agents.
records.ofc_text: Install protective measures, such as screens, to protect against malicious acts. Screens will prevent objects from being tossed into the intakes from the ground; such screens should be sloped to allow thrown objects to roll or slide off the screen, away from intakes.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 3.4, p. 3-35 to 3-44, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.vulnerability: The facility has multiple communications service connections, but they enter at the same geographic location, causing a potential single point of failure for facility communications.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the communications service connections so they enter the facility in different locations. This will reduce the likelihood that an incident in one location would disrupt all facility communications systems.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-38 to F-39, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Are external communications required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the communications service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Determine the communications service requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not participate in a plan with the communications service provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the service provider to determine if the company prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Are external communications required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals
records.vulnerability: The chemical most critical to facility core operations is not available from multiple suppliers. In the event that the provider is unable to provide this chemical, facility operations may be severely impacted.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the provider to become better informed about the company's business continuity management program, notification procedures for supply disruptions, and the overall resilience of the supply system. Use this information to improve the facility's own business continuity plans, policies, and procedures, as necessary.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the need to store this critical chemical onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the primary provider. As part of the evaluation, consider potential requirements associated with storage of hazardous chemicals subject to Federal security regulations.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals

records.child_node: Is there a Contingency / Business Continuity Plan with provider(s)?

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the provider(s) of the chemical most critical to facility core operations for rapid restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.
records.ofc_text: Determine the supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider(s) to ensure delivery is restored with priority, or alternate provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in a supply chain disruption and corresponding timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals
records.ofc_text: Provide adequate lead time or use automated ordering processes to maintain adequate supplies of this critical chemical.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #2) Evaluate the need to store this critical chemical onsite to mitigate the impacts of a disruption in deliveries from the primary provider. As part of the evaluation, consider potential requirements associated with storage of hazardous chemicals subject to Federal security regulations.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.child_node: Does the facility participate in provider priority plan for restoration?

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals
records.vulnerability: The facility does not participate in a plan with the provider(s) of the chemical most critical to facility core operations for priority restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the provider(s) to determine if they prioritize certain types of customers for restoration of deliveries before other customers in the event of a supply disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Chemicals
records.ofc_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration of deliveries before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a supply disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the provider(s) to review options for rapid restoration of deliveries. Establish a liaison with the provider(s) to maintain communications during a supply disruption. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Byproducts/wastes (e.g., sulfur, garbage)
records.ofc_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration of service before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the provider(s) to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the provider(s) to maintain communications during a service disruption. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Raw Materials
records.vulnerability: The raw material most critical to facility core operations is not available from multiple suppliers. In the event that the provider is unable to provide this material, facility operations may be severely impacted.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #1) Collaborate with the provider to become better informed about the company's business continuity management program, notification procedures for supply disruptions, and the overall resilience of the supply system. Use this information to improve the facility's own business continuity plans, policies, and procedures, as necessary.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.child_node: Is there a Contingency / Business Continuity Plan with provider(s)?

records.parent_question: Critical Products: Raw Materials
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the provider(s) of the raw material most critical to facility core operations for rapid restoration of deliveries in the event of a supply disruption.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #3) Determine the supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the provider(s) to ensure delivery is restored with priority, or alternate provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in a supply chain disruption and corresponding timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management - Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.7, p. 24. ; Protiviti, 2006, Guide To Business Continuity Management Frequently Asked Questions, 2nd Edition, p. 40-46, http://www.protiviti.com/en-US/Documents/Resource-Guides/bcm_faq_booklet.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Provide adequate lead time or use automated ordering processes to maintain adequate supplies of this critical material.

records.vulnerability: At the time of the visit, the substation(s) servicing the facility was unknown. This lack of knowledge may be indicative of a lack of communication between the facility and the electric power utility provider.
records.ofc_text: Reach out to the utility provider to determine the substation(s) that services the facility. Maintaining regular communication channels with utility service providers will enhance the facility's understanding of actions to be taken in response to a loss of service from primary supply sources, restoration priorities, and potential alternatives.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.child_node: How many electric service connections are there for the facility?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility has only one electric service connection, creating a potential single point of failure in its electric power supply.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the electric power utility provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy. It is preferred that a secondary connection be serviced from a different substation and that the connection be able to support the entire load of the facility.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.child_node: If more than one, can each service connection handle entire facility load?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: Neither electric service connection for the facility is capable of handling the entire facility load. Therefore, if service from one connection was disrupted, the electric power supply to the facility would be degraded.
records.ofc_text: Install an emergency generator capable of providing adequate power for the entire facility load during an extended commercial power outage.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with electric power utility provider to explore options to modify service connections such that any one connection could meet the entire facility load.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: All electric service connections for the facility enter at the same geographic location, causing a potential single point of failure in the electric power supply for the facility.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the electric service connections so they enter the facility at different locations.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.child_node: Are the service connections collocated with other utilities (e.g., utility corridors for natural gas, communications, fiber, water, etc.)?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The electric power service connection(s) for the facility is collocated with other utilities (e.g., in utility corridors for natural gas, communications, fiber, water): therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt the operations of multiple service systems.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the electric service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.child_node: Are there protective measures in place inside the facility supporting the electrical system (e.g., locked electrical cabinet or room)?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: There are no protective measures in place for electrical system components inside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.
records.ofc_text: Secure components of the electrical system inside the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets and locked electrical boxes.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for electrical system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.child_node: Are there protective measures in place outside the building supporting the electrical system (but still within control of the facility, e.g., bollards or box around facility-owned transformer)?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: There are no protective measures in place for electrical system components outside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.
records.ofc_text: Secure components of the electrical system outside of the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with door locks.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-89, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Install a fence or protective structure around exterior electrical system equipment (e.g., transformers, switchgear) to secure them against unauthorized access and to decrease the risk of accidental or intentional damage to these critical components.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27 to 2-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for components of the electrical system, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27 to 2-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Conduct random security checks of critical exterior electrical system equipment.

records.child_node: Is there a contingency/business continuity plan with provider(s) for restoration?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the electric power utility provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Determine the electric power requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.child_node: Does the facility participate in provider priority plan for restoration?

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not participate in a plan with the electric power utility provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have an alternate or backup that can be used in the event of a loss of commercial power. A loss of electric power could significantly disrupt facility operations.
records.ofc_text: Evaluate power requirements for the facility to maintain core operations, and install backup power equipment accordingly. At a minimum, ensure all life safety services have backup power.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Conduct an assessment to determine which systems and equipment are essential to the continued operation of the facility. They may include one or more of the following: HVAC systems; industrial equipment and major appliances; lights (interior and exterior), computers, and other office equipment; pumps, including sump pumps, sprinkler system pumps, and well water pumps; and alarm systems. Once essential systems and equipment have been identified, determine how much power they require, and then consult with a generator technical expert regarding the appropriate size and type of generator(s) for the facility. The technical expert can also help select other components of the emergency power system, including the main transfer switch and the electrical panel.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Install a Generator for Emergency Power, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Explore options to enter into a contract to have a backup power generator readily available, if installing a generator onsite is not feasible. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Install a Generator for Emergency Power, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: If the installation of three or more backup power generators is planned, consider installing them in two separate locations to mitigate the vulnerability of a single point of failure. Similarly, the electric utility switchgear, the automatic transfer switch, and the backup switchgear should be in separate locations with sufficient separation distance.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.2.2, p. 3-26, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external electric power required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.2.2, p. 3-26, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.child_node: Does the facility report cybersecurity incidents to outside organizations?

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not report cybersecurity incidents to outside organizations. Information sharing with other entities increases cyber resilience.
records.ofc_text: Report all cyber incidents to appropriate outside organizations. Cyber incident reports can be made to request technical assistance, to request incident management support, to inform law enforcement, and/or for information sharing.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 33, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Report all cyber incidents to appropriate outside organizations. Cyber incident reports can be made to request technical assistance, request incident management support, and/or for information sharing. Information sharing plays a key role in the prevention of cyber attacks and ensures new threats can be identified quickly and effective countermeasures can be developed to neutralize/defeat them. Cyber threats/attack techniques are continuously evolving and change on a daily basis; many organizations/companies may be unaware of these latest threats. Participating in information sharing with other outside organizations and facilities may reduce this vulnerability and could possibly prevent a future cybersecurity attack from being successful.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 33, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Access control policies: access control is necessary to ensure that use of the system or information is provided only to authorized users who have identified and authenticated themselves to obtain access. Consult NIST Interagency Report 7316: Assessment of Access Control Systems, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistir/7316/NISTIR-7316.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2014, "Role Based Access Control (RBAC) and Role Based Security," http://csrc.nist.gov/groups/SNS/rbac/, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: IT security roles and responsibilities: Defining clear roles and responsibilities is necessary to ensure that cyber security is properly managed and executed, that all necessary duties are assigned, that personnel receive the proper training, and that personnel have only the privileges necessary to fulfill their assignments.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 1995, "Roles & Responsibilities," NIST SP 800-12: An Introduction to Computer Security - The NIST Handbook, October, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-12/800-12-html/chapter3.html, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: IT security training: Personnel need sufficient training, based upon their roles and responsibilities, to ensure they can perform their jobs without introducing security vulnerabilities. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24-25, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Audit trails: Without an audit trail, an organization might fail to detect security compromises, misuse of information, or other security-related events. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Disposal of protected assets: Sensitive information must be removed using proper sanitation methods, or media destruction, to ensure information is not released during disposal of protected assets. Consult NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2013, NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, April, http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Incident response/management: This element captures the means of the facility to detect and respond to five categories of cyber incidents: Unauthorized Access, DOS, Malicious Code, Improper Usage, and Scans/Probes/Attempted Access. The incidents listed can be detected by multiple technical means including intrusion detection/prevention systems, firewalls, anti-virus tools, and vulnerability detection/assessment tools. Managing responses to incidents is necessary to ensure that incidents are properly detected and reported so that the incident is handled properly, forensic evidence is not lost, and essential operations are recovered as soon as possible.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Unauthorized access, i.e., an individual gains logical or physical access without permission to a network, system, application, data, or other resource. Unauthorized access may result in release of sensitive information or disruption of business operations.

records.ofc_text: DOS, i.e., an attack that successfully prevents or impairs the normal authorized functionality of networks, systems, or applications by exhausting resources. This activity includes being the victim or participating in the DOS. Defending against DOS attacks can benefit from detecting the presence of an attack and receiving external support to block incoming network traffic.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 25-29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Security testing: Testing is necessary to verify and validate that the intended security rules are properly implemented. Vulnerabilities include software holes that a hacker might take advantage of, out of date virus definition files, default passwords set by a vendor, or accounts that are not password protected. Software designed for scanning, such as ISS or Nessus, is commonly used, as is active scan systems, which detect vulnerabilities as soon as a system joins the network.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Physical security of IT security assets: Without proper physical and environmental security, information systems are vulnerable to intentional attacks or accidents that can disruption their operations and release sensitive information.

Firewalls: Firewalls are devices or programs that control the flow of network traffic between networks or hosts that employ differing security postures. Without a firewall, internal systems are exposed to potential attacks against which they might not have sufficient protection. Consult NIST SP 800-41: Guidelines on Firewalls and 
Firewall Policy, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-41-Rev1/sp800-41-rev1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text:
Remote access: Remote access introduces numerous vulnerabilities ranging from lack of physical security on remote access devices (home computers, phones, tables), potential malware on those remote devices, information travels over unsecured networks (Internet), and opening internal resources to external devices. Securing remote access requires encrypting traffic from the remote devices, using technologies such as VPN, and strictly enforcing authentication. Consult NIST SP 800-46: Guide to Enterprise Telework 
and Remote Access Security, available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-46-rev1/sp800-46r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Wireless connectivity: Wireless technologies are typically less secure than wired technologies, primarily because they can extend outside of the physical footprint of the organization, but also because they often have weak security configurations. A best-case scenario would be for wireless to not be used at all, especially on control networks. Other scenarios may have a separate visitor network space, such that a visitor would not be able to scan traffic on the internal network. Employees would need to VPN or authenticate in some manner to gain access. A worst-case scenario would be that wireless is open to all, and exists on the same network as the internal systems. Consult NIST SP 800-153: Guidelines for Securing Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs), available at http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-153/sp800-153.pdf, for more information on securing wireless technology.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Security patches or updates: A "patch" is a piece of computer code that a software company writes and distributes to fix a problem found in one of its previously released programs. Many patches fix problems related to security—specifically, vulnerabilities in the programs that attackers can exploit. Hackers seek out these vulnerabilities to gain access to a computer and its information, which then can be used to gain access to other vulnerable computers and information. These compromised computers also can be used to attack other computers. To prevent these problems, patches need to be deployed to computer systems quickly to minimize the window of opportunity for attackers. Consult NIST SP 800-40 Revision 3: Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Technologies, available at http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-40r3.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.child_node: Does the facility conduct cybersecurity exercises for purposes of training, system testing, continuity planning, or disaster recovery?

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not conduct cybersecurity exercises for purposes of training, system testing, continuity planning, or disaster recovery. The periodic performance of exercises and testing helps to ensure the security protections perform as expected, or corrected if not, and personnel understand system and security controls.
records.ofc_text: Conduct regular cybersecurity exercises. An exercise is a simulation of an emergency designed to validate the viability of one or more aspects of an IT plan. In an exercise, personnel with roles and responsibilities in a particular IT plan meet to validate the content of a plan through discussion of their roles and their responses to emergency situations, execution of responses in a simulated operational environment, or other means of validating responses that does not involve using the actual operational environment. Exercises are scenario-driven, such as a power failure in one of the organization’s data centers or a fire causing certain systems to be damaged, with additional situations often being presented during the course of an exercise.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Document the results from cybersecurity exercises. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and the adequacy of cybersecurity response policies and procedures in the event of a real cybersecurity attack. Modify cybersecurity plans, policies, and procedures as necessary.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not perform security scans on the IT network, which may allow vulnerabilities and weaknesses in the network to remain undetected.
records.ofc_text: Implement the capability to scan the network for vulnerabilities and weaknesses. Implement a process for addressing the identified issues.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 30-31, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Implement the capability to scan the network for vulnerabilities and weaknesses. Implement a process for addressing the identified issues. Determine the frequency and comprehensiveness of vulnerability scans based on the security categorization of the facility's information systems, and ensure that potential sources of vulnerabilities such as networked printers, scanners, and copiers are not overlooked. Vulnerability analyses for custom software applications may require additional approaches such as static analysis, dynamic analysis, binary analysis, or a hybrid of the three approaches. Organizations can employ these analysis approaches in a variety of tools (e.g., Web-based application scanners, static analysis tools, binary analyzers) and in source code reviews.

records.child_node: Is there network segmentation between control networks and business networks?

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility has no network segmentation between control networks and business networks. Network segmentation isolates critical assets from other less critical assets to minimize the reach of cyber attackers within the network.
records.ofc_text: Implement LAN segmentation physically or with VLANs. Use routing to provide systems cross-connectivity only where necessary. Employ access control measures to restrict access between segmented networks. Managed interfaces include, for example, gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis and virtualization systems, and encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture (e.g., routers protecting firewalls or application gateways residing on protected subnetworks). Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational information systems includes, for example, restricting external Web traffic to designated Web servers within managed interfaces and prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses. Consider the shared nature of commercial telecommunications services in the implementation of security controls associated with the use of such services. Commercial telecommunications services are commonly based on network components and consolidated management systems shared by all attached commercial customers, and may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such transmission services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2013, NIST SP 800-53 Revision 4: Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations, p. F-188, April, http://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-53r4.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NIST, 2010, "Technical Contingency Planning Considerations," NIST SP 800-34 Revision 1: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, May, http://csrc.nist.gov/publications/nistpubs/800-34-rev1/sp800-34-rev1_errata-Nov11-2010.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.
