records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have redundant, separated critical servers or network components.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing redundant critical servers and/or network components preferably in different buildings or locations at a reasonably significant distance apart. This will reduce the likelihood that an incident in one location would disrupt the operations of these critical assets.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.child_node: Does the facility use Backup Data Storage?

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not use backup data storage, leaving it susceptible to the loss of critical and possibly irretrievable data and information.
records.ofc_text: Establish a backup data storage site, preferably at an offsite or separate location, to prevent the loss of critical data and information.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Store backup tapes at either an alternate location or a different room in a fireproof safe. In the event of an incident in the server room, the information on the damaged or destroyed servers would remain intact.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: Conduct a feasibility study to duplicate the internal IT system at another location to backup critical data and information in real-time.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 29, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.child_node: Is access to control/computer rooms and remote equipment controlled?

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: Access to control/computer rooms and remote equipment is not controlled.
records.ofc_text: Implement access control policies and procedures to secure control/computer rooms and remote equipment. Access control measures to consider include door locks, card readers, intrusion detection sensors, and CCTV.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 23-24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the IT service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the IT service provider to establish a contingency/business continuity plan for restoration.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.child_node: Does the facility participates in provider priority plan for restoration?

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not participate in a plan with the IT service provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the IT service provider to determine if the company prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 26-28, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is Information Technology required for Facility core operations (e.g., produce key services/goods)?
records.ofc_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
records.reference: NIST, 2014, Framework for Improving Critical Infrastructure Cybersecurity, p. 24, February 12, http://www.nist.gov/cyberframework/upload/cybersecurity-framework-021214.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the natural gas utility provider on the feasibility of modifying service connections such that any one connection could meet the entire facility load.

records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the natural gas service connections so that they enter the facility at different locations.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.vulnerability: The natural gas service connection(s) for the facility is collocated with other utilities (e.g., in utility corridors for electric power, communications, fiber, water): therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt the operations of multiple service systems.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the natural gas service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.vulnerability: There are no protective measures in place for natural gas supply system components inside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.
records.ofc_text: Secure components of the natural gas system inside of the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for natural gas system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.ofc_text: Conduct random security checks of critical interior utility closets and assets.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), Appendix F, p. F-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Secure components of the natural gas system outside of the facility against unauthorized access.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #6) Evaluate the need to implement enhanced protective measures for natural gas system components, such as intrusion detection sensors and CCTV.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.ofc_text: Conduct random security checks of critical exterior natural gas system equipment.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.vulnerability: The natural gas main and associated equipment is unprotected from accidental or intentional vehicle damage.
records.ofc_text: Install vehicle barriers to protect the natural gas main and associated equipment from damage.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-27, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external natural gas required for the Facility for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?_x000D_
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near the natural gas main and associated equipment. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.
records.reference: FEMA, 2005, Risk Management Series – Risk Assessment: A How-To Guide to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 452), Appendix A, p. A-11, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1524-20490-7395/fema452_01_05.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Determine the natural gas supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #3) Include in the facility's business continuity plan immediate, interim, and long-term mitigation measures/strategies for responding to the loss of this critical transportation mode.
records.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18.

records.parent_question: Mode: Pipeline

records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the wastewater utility provider on the feasibility of installing a second service connection that enters the facility in a different location to create redundancy.

records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the wastewater utility provider on the feasibility of implementing infrastructure upgrades to mitigate potential impacts from the disruption of one of the wastewater service connections to the facility.

records.parent_question: Does the facility require external wastewater discharge services for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: All wastewater service connections for the facility enter at the same geographic location, creating a potential single point of failure in the wastewater removal service supply for the facility.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of separating the wastewater service connections so they enter the facility at different locations.

records.parent_question: Does the facility require external wastewater discharge services for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: The wastewater service connection(s) for the facility is collocated with other utilities (e.g., in utility corridors for electric power, communications, fiber, natural gas): therefore, an incident in one location could disrupt the operations of multiple service systems.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of relocating the wastewater service connection(s) to help mitigate the risk that the catastrophic failure of one utility could inadvertently take down another.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility require external wastewater discharge services for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: There are no protective measures in place for wastewater service system components inside the facility building(s), leaving them at risk for tampering and accidental or intentional damage.
records.ofc_text: Secure components of the wastewater system inside the facility against unauthorized access, for example, with access-controlled utility closets.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 3.6, p. 3-87 to 3-91, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility require external wastewater discharge services for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?

records.parent_question: Does the facility require external wastewater discharge services for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the wastewater service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Determine the wastewater service requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility require external wastewater discharge services for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not participate in a plan with the wastewater service utility provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility require external wastewater discharge services for Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.ofc_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.ofc_text: Secure manhole covers 10 inches or more in diameter, for example, with locks and hasps, by welding them shut, or by appropriate bolting to their frames. Ensure that hasps, locks, and bolts are made of materials that resist corrosion. Keyed bolts (which make removal by unauthorized personnel more difficult) are also available.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?

records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Install vehicle barriers to protect the water supply from damage.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Site and Urban Design for Security (FEMA 430), section 5.12, p. 5-33, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of prohibiting parking near the water supply. Utility systems should be located at least 50 feet from loading docks, front entrances, and parking areas.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a contingency/business continuity plan with the water service provider for rapid restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Determine the water supply requirements to support facility core operations, and develop commensurate plans in collaboration with the utility provider to ensure service is restored with priority, or alternate service provisions are made. Ensure plans address legitimate hypothetical scenarios that would result in service loss and corresponding restoration timelines so the facility can address business continuity and interim workarounds.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not participate in a plan with the water service utility provider for priority restoration in the event of a service disruption.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the utility provider to determine if the utility prioritizes certain types of facilities for restoration before other customers in the event of a service disruption, and if the facility is eligible for priority restoration.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.ofc_text: If the facility is not eligible for priority restoration before other customers, establish a contingency/business continuity plan to resume facility operations after a service disruption. As part of the planning process, collaborate with the utility provider to review options for rapid restoration of service. Establish a liaison with the utility provider to maintain communications during a service outage. Ensure the contingency/business continuity plan is well understood by facility management and employees.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have an alternate source of water to use as a backup in the event of a disruption to or loss of the primary water supply.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the water utility service provider to evaluate alternative water supply options. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider when planning and implementing such contingencies.
records.reference: FEMA, 2010, Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans: Comprehensive Preparedness Guide 101, p. C-13 to C-14, November, http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.ofc_text: Conduct a water audit to understand the facility's water usage, then evaluate alternative water supply options. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider throughout the duration of this process.
records.reference: CDC and American Water Works Association, 2012, Emergency Water Supply Planning Guide for Hospitals and Health Care Facilities, http://www.cdc.gov/healthywater/pdf/emergency/emergency-water-supply-planning-guide.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?
records.ofc_text: Conduct a water audit to understand the facility's water usage, then evaluate alternative water supply options. Based on analysis of the water use audit and the availability of alternative water supply options, develop a written emergency water supply plan for the facility. Complete activities necessary to ensure the availability of alternate emergency water supplies. Practice and exercise the emergency water supply plan. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider throughout the duration of this process.
records.reference: CDC and American Water Works Association, 2012, Emergency Water Supply Planning Guide for Hospitals and Health Care Facilities, http://www.cdc.gov/healthywater/pdf/emergency/emergency-water-supply-planning-guide.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is external water required for the Facility Core Operations (Produce Key Services, Goods)?

records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the water utility service provider to evaluate alternative water supply options for the fire suppression system. It is critical to engage with the water utility service provider when planning and implementing such contingencies.
records.reference: CDC and American Water Works Association, 2012, Emergency Water Supply Planning Guide for Hospitals and Health Care Facilities, http://www.cdc.gov/healthywater/pdf/emergency/emergency-water-supply-planning-guide.pdf, accessed June 26, 2014.

Does the facility utilize an exterior intrusion detection system (IDS)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have an exterior IDS. An exterior IDS can provide the facility with increased capabilities for detection of attempted and successful exterior security breaches, allowing for more rapid response to intrusions and thereby preventing further damage to the facility.
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. Types of sensors used to detect exterior intrusion include buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 97-113.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize an exterior intrusion detection system (IDS)?
records.ofc_text:
Evaluate the weakest areas of the exterior and install IDS sensors as appropriate. The basic
types of sensors used for exterior intrusion are buried-line sensors, fence-associated sensors, and freestanding sensors. The IDS should be evenly distributed throughout critical areas to ensure uniform detection around the entire length of the facility’s perimeter. Sensors should be placed in locations that avoid electromagnetic, acoustic, thermal, nuclear radiation, optical, seismic, and meteorological interferences.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 97-113, p. 286-287.

records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 97-113, p. 286-287.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize an exterior intrusion detection system (IDS)?
records.child_node: Tamper and system problem indicators provided
records.vulnerability: The exterior IDS lacks tampering and system issue indicators. Without such indicators, tampering attempts and system issues will likely remain undetected by the facility.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of incorporating tampering and system issue indicators to the IDS to ensure facility personnel are notified of incidents that could render the IDS nonoperational.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 99-100.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize an exterior intrusion detection system (IDS)?
records.child_node: Detection zone kept clear of obstructions (e.g., dips, equipment, snow, ice, grass, debris)
records.vulnerability: The detection zone is not kept clear of obstructions (e.g., dips, equipment, snow, ice, grass, debris).
records.ofc_text: Establish a clear detection zone. Perimeter IDS systems perform more effectively when located in a defined clear zone. Such areas should be cleared of aboveground obstacles and vegetation that might block the IDS receivers and transmitters.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 99-100.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize an exterior intrusion detection system (IDS)?

records.vulnerability: The facility does not employ compensatory measures when the exterior IDS alarms are nonoperational to detect attempted and successful security breaches.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #6) Plan to increase security measures when the IDS is not operational, for example, increased roving patrols by the security force and/or additional access control measures.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 99-100.

Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 281-285; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Officer Operations, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 51-52.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not maintain the exterior IDS according to recommended specifications. Without regular maintenance, the system may fall into disrepair and its overall effectiveness may be degraded.
records.ofc_text: Maintain the IDS according to the manufacturer's recommend specifications. Develop a maintenance log to document all repairs and part replacements for the IDS.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 156; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 92-97.

Does the facility utilize an exterior intrusion detection system (IDS)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not test the exterior IDS periodically. Failure to periodically test the IDS could allow deficiencies in the system to remain unaddressed and reduce its overall effectiveness.
records.ofc_text: Develop policies and procedures to periodically test the IDS and address inadequate test results. Maintain records of test results, issues, and the resolution of identified issues.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 154-156.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not have an interior IDS to detect attempted and successful security breaches.
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the weakest points of entry into the facility and install IDS sensors as appropriate. Types of sensors used to detect interior intrusion include boundary penetration sensors, interior motion sensors, proximity sensors, door sensors, and window sensors.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not maintain the interior IDS according to recommended specifications. Without regular maintenance, the system may fall into disrepair and its overall effectiveness may be degraded.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #12) Maintain the IDS according to the manufacturer's recommend specifications. Develop a maintenance log to document all repairs and part replacements for the IDS.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 156; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 92-97.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not test the interior IDS periodically. Failure to periodically test the IDS could allow deficiencies in the system to remain unaddressed and reduce its overall effectiveness.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #13) Develop policies and procedures to periodically test the IDS and address inadequate test results. Maintain records of test results, issues, and the resolution of identified issues.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 154-156.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize an interior intrusion detection system?
Does the facility utilize an interior intrusion detection system?
Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of installing a comprehensive CCTV system onsite.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3, p. 5-44 to 5-50, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of installing a comprehensive CCTV system onsite. Site-specific factors must be considered when selecting components that comprise a particular CCTV system. For example, the size of the system, in terms of the number of cameras needed, is the minimum number required to view all electronic security system sensor detection fields. In addition, some cameras may require artificial light sources. Finally, performance criteria and physical, environmental, and economic considerations must be factored into the component selection.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3, p. 5-44 to 5-50, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.ofc_text: Install CCTV cameras where they will capture an unobstructed field of view of the desired scene and where there is minimal risk of vandalism, sabotage, or environmental damage, if possible.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the need to install CCTV cameras in the following locations outdoors: along site perimeter detection zone, at controlled access points, within the protected area, and to view approaches to critical assets.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46 to 5-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the need to install CCTV cameras in the following locations indoors: at selected assets within the protected area, and at critical portals adjacent to assets.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46 to 5-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.ofc_text: Provide for effective monitoring of the CCTV system based on the security requirements of the facility.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46 to 5-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the need for dedicated, trained security staff to monitor the CCTV system. Explore options to maximize the effectiveness of CCTV monitoring and observation, such as frequently rotating shifts for monitoring staff and limiting the number of cameras monitored by each staff member.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.2, p. 5-46 to 5-47, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.ofc_text: Determine the objectives of video recording and retention (e.g., verify information, prove it, or aid prosecution with it) to select the appropriate video recording and storage systems for the facility.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Buildings and Infrastructure Protection Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS-06), section 5.5.3.3, p. 5-49, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility utilize CCTV?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a CCTV system. CCTV systems provide a rapid and cost-effective method for determining the source of intrusion or other detection alarms, if they are in place, and support surveillance of activities and events within and around a facility. In addition, a well-designed CCTV system provides a cost-effective supplement to security force patrols.

records.child_node: No   (If No, PSA should provide flyer or information on program)
records.vulnerability: Facility personnel are not aware of the nationwide "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. This program is intended to raise public awareness of indicators of terrorism and terrorism-related crime and to emphasize the importance of reporting suspicious activity to the proper authorities.
records.ofc_text: Consult your local PSA for information about the "If You See Something, Say Something™" public awareness campaign. For more information, visit the DHS Website at http://www.dhs.gov/if-you-see-something-say-something%E2%84%A2-campaign.

Does your facility use some standard to guide your risk management activities?
records.child_node: Do not utilize any type of standard
records.vulnerability: The facility does not use standards to guide its risk management activities. There are a number of established business continuity, emergency management, and risk management standards available that define the overall approach to the successful management of risk, describe the risk management process, and suggest a framework to support that process. The use of such standards could enhance the facility's current risk management program.
records.ofc_text: Research, review, and evaluate established risk management standards. Select the approach that is most relevant to the facility's particular circumstances.
records.reference: Biringer, B.E., R.V. Matalucci, and S.L. O’Connor, 2007, Security Risk Assessment and Management – A Professional Practice Guide for Protecting Buildings and Infrastructures, Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons, p. 175.

records.reference: Biringer, B.E., R.V. Matalucci, and S.L. O’Connor, 2007, Security Risk Assessment and Management – A Professional Practice Guide for Protecting Buildings and Infrastructures, Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons, p. 175.

records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #9) In May 2014, DHS announced the adoption of the following updated preparedness standards: ASIS International SPC.1-2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use Standards, ISO 22301:2012: Societal security -- Business continuity management systems, and NFPA 1600: Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs, 2013 edition More information about these standards is available through the FEMA Website, at http://www.fema.gov/about-ps-preptm.
records.reference: Biringer, B.E., R.V. Matalucci, and S.L. O’Connor, 2007, Security Risk Assessment and Management – A Professional Practice Guide for Protecting Buildings and Infrastructures, Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons, p. 175.

Law Enforcement Agency
records.child_node: Is there a written MOU/MOA with this first responder [not just 911]?

records.parent_question: Law Enforcement Agency
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a written MOU/MOA with the primary law enforcement agency. Formal agreements with first responders expedite assistance and the provision of special services as necessary.
records.ofc_text: Establish an MOU/MOA with the agency to define the assistance and special services the agency will provide in the event of a threat, attack, or incident. An MOU/MOA can address items such as points of contact, notification procedures, exchange of threat information, access to the facility, hazards present on the facility, and participation in regular consults, drills, and exercises.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

records.parent_question: Law Enforcement Agency
records.ofc_text: Establish a liaison with the agency. Explore the option of creating a formal agreement for assistance and special services.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

records.parent_question: Law Enforcement Agency
Law Enforcement Agency
records.child_node: Have there been onsite visit(s) with this first responder?

records.parent_question: Law Enforcement Agency
records.vulnerability: The primary law enforcement agency has not conducted an onsite visit of the facility. Onsite visits can familiarize first responders with the facility, key personnel, site layout, and other issues that would enhance incident response.
records.ofc_text: Invite the agency to visit the facility. Provide a tour to familiarize first responders with the site layout.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

records.parent_question: Law Enforcement Agency
records.ofc_text: Establish annual onsite visits for first responders to maintain their familiarity with the facility.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

records.child_node: Is there Interoperable Communication with this first responder  [not 911]?

records.parent_question: Law Enforcement Agency
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have interoperable communications with the primary law enforcement agency. Without interoperable communications, the facility cannot communicate with first responders in order to coordinate security and response activities.
records.ofc_text: Collaborate with the agency on potential solutions to achieve cost-effective interoperable communications onsite.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 285-286.

records.parent_question: Law Enforcement Agency
records.ofc_text: Explore the option of an interconnect system, such as a gateway that can allow communication between radio systems that are otherwise incompatible because they operate on different frequency bands or using different technologies.

records.ofc_text: The CDC’s PHIN is a national initiative to increase the capacity of public health agencies to electronically exchange data and information across organizations and jurisdictions (e.g., clinical care to public health, public health to public health and public health to other federal agencies). Contact PHIN by phone or email, at 770-454-4863 or PHINTECH@CDC.GOV, or visit the CDC Website for more information, at http://www.cdc.gov/phin/about/index.html.

records.ofc_text: The CDC Office for State, Tribal, Local and Territorial Support includes the CoPs Program: CoPs provide a collaborative framework for public health professionals to share information and expertise. To join a CoP, sign up at http://www.phconnect.org or visit the Join a CoP Webpage, at http://www.cdc.gov/phcommunities/join.html.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate hurricane impacts.
records.ofc_text: Install permanent storm shutters to protect windows to help prevent glass breakage and stop windborne objects from penetrating the facility. Always use permanent storm shutters in areas where there will be little advance warning of high winds, as permanent shutters can be closed quickly, unlike temporary plywood covers that must be retrieved from storage and mounted with bolts or screws. Consult the FEMA flyer Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to hurricane-related flooding.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore flood-proofing retrofits such as dry floodproofing (i.e., measures resulting in watertight, flood-resistant structures), wet flood-proofing (i.e., the use of flood-damage-resistant materials and construction techniques to minimize flood damage), floodwalls, and levees. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Relocate utility installations and other critical assets away from rooms and locations that are below-grade and susceptible to flooding-related damage.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. If possible, move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from severe weather events such as wind. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of using natural gas to fuel the backup power system. While diesel, which is traditionally used to fuel backup power systems, is relatively safe to store onsite and economical to procure, re-supply for an extended period may be an issue. Because most natural gas infrastructure is underground, the natural gas supply may not be impacted by severe weather events.
records.reference: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during hurricane season.
records.ofc_text: Establish specific plans and procedures for the duration of hurricane season. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available hurricane planning resources.
records.reference: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Develop shelter-in-place plans for facility occupants if it is not safe to evacuate the facility during a natural disaster. Train employees on the plan, and conduct recurring exercises on, reviews of, and updates to the plan. Ensure contractors and frequent visitors to the facility are aware of these plans. Consult the Ready.gov Website, available at http://www.ready.gov/shelter, for more information on sheltering.
records.reference: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Establish a hurricane planning checklist that can be provided to all facility occupants ahead of hurricane season.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Develop a plan for dealing with power outages and loss of elevator service in high-rise buildings. Ensure the plan includes the location of alternate exits for occupants.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Ensure all trees on facility property are far enough away to prevent them from damaging the facility if they should fall. Consult the FEMA flyer Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a hurricane.
records.ofc_text: If the facility does not have permanent storm shutters, establish plans to board windows with exterior-grade or marine plywood. Ensure temporary covers are readily accessible, and use a numbering or lettering system that shows which cover goes with which window.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Establish plans to deploy emergency flood mitigation measures, such as sandbags, temporary flood barriers, and flood wrapping systems. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate flood impacts.
records.ofc_text: Conduct a vulnerability assessment for electrical and mechanical utilities and systems to understand the facility's susceptibility to damage due to severe flooding.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #5) Explore flood-proofing retrofits such as dry floodproofing (i.e., measures resulting in watertight, flood-resistant structures), wet flood-proofing (i.e., the use of flood-damage-resistant materials and construction techniques to minimize flood damage), floodwalls, and levees. Consult Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Floodproofing Non-Residential Buildings (FEMA P-936), section 2.6.3, p. 2-48 to 2-51, July, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/9a50c534fc5895799321dcdd4b6083e7/P-936_8-20-13_508r.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #6) Relocate utility installations and other critical assets away from rooms and locations that are below-grade and susceptible to flooding-related damage.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank, as well as electrical and fuel supply lines, from destructive natural hazards. In flood hazard areas, mount the generator and tank securely on concrete platforms, above the expected flood level. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #8) Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #9) Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 2.4.6, p. 2-85, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #10) Explore the feasibility of using natural gas to fuel the backup power system. While diesel, which is traditionally used to fuel backup power systems, is relatively safe to store onsite and economical to procure, re-supply for an extended period may be an issue. Because most natural gas infrastructure is underground, the natural gas supply may not be impacted by severe weather events.
records.reference: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #23) Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/floods, for more information.
records.reference: Hamilton, R., 2007, Power topic #7006 -Lessons in emergency power preparedness: Planning in the wake of Katrina, Cummins Power Generation Inc., p.3, November, http://www.cumminspower.com/www/literature/technicalpapers/PT-7006-Standby-Katrina-en.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014; ICF International, 2013, Combined Heat and Power: Enabling Resilient Energy Infrastructure for Critical Facilities, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, p. 6, March, http://www1.eere.energy.gov/manufacturing/distributedenergy/pdfs/chp_critical_facilities.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate earthquake impacts.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of modifying or retrofitting the facility to make it safer during earthquakes and more resistant to earthquake damage and disruption. The optimal combination earthquake risk-reduction measures for a facility will depend on factors such as the potential severity of the earthquake hazards in the area, the current condition of the facility, whether the facility is owned or leased, and how vulnerable facility operations are to facility damage and associated downtime. Consult QuakeSmart, at http://www.fema.gov/quakesmart, for information about how to assess facility risks and how to develop and implement a plan to cost-effectively mitigate those risks.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: If the facility has cripple walls, brace the walls by adding 2-inch by 4-inch boards between vertical studs at the top and bottom of the walls and, if necessary, at other locations between the studs. Consult the FEMA flyer Brace Cripple Walls, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0271/how2012_brace_cripple_walls.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Brace Cripple Walls, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0271/how2012_brace_cripple_walls.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Anchor any sill plates. Sill plates rest directly on top of a foundation, and bolting the plates directly into the foundation provides extra stability. Consult the FEMA flyer Bolt Sill Plates to Foundation, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-5339/how2013_sill_plates.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Bolt Sill Plates to Foundation, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-5339/how2013_sill_plates.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of retrofitting existing gas or water lines with flexible connections to prevent damage. Consult the FEMA flyer Use Flexible Connection on Gas and Water Lines, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-7131/how2014_flex_connections.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Use Flexible Connection on Gas and Water Lines, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-7131/how2014_flex_connections.pdf.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of anchoring heavy equipment directly to the floor or another suitable part of the building. If this is not possible, mount equipment on vibration isolators. Consult the FEMA flyer Anchor Equipment Properly, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Anchor Equipment Properly, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #8) Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Anchor Equipment Properly, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #9) Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Anchor Equipment Properly, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Anchor Equipment Properly, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-9548/how2023_anchor_equip.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for earthquakes.
records.ofc_text: Establish earthquake emergency plans and procedures. Emphasize safe places for building occupants to "drop, cover, and hold on" during earthquake shaking. Establish safe locations where people can rendezvous when the shaking has stopped and it is safe and advisable to evacuate the facility. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available earthquake planning resources.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Conduct periodic, mandatory earthquake drills for employees to practice earthquake response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Arrange for employees to be trained in first aid, CPR, and fire extinguisher use to help protect facility occupants in the immediate aftermath of an earthquake.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore opportunities to engage in Great ShakeOut Earthquake Drills, which are annual events for communities to collectively practice and improve earthquake preparedness. Consult the Great ShakeOut Earthquake Drills Website, at http://www.shakeout.org, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, "Earthquake Safety at Work," January 18, http://www.fema.gov/earthquake/earthquake-safety-work, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Anchor overhead lighting fixtures, brace top-heavy objects, fasten all wall objects securely, and place all large or heavy objects in low positions (e.g., on the floor, lower shelves).
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Fact Sheet: Earthquakes, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1621-20490-0014/earthquakesfactsheet_final.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate tornado impacts.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing a safe room or rooms specifically designed to meet FEMA criteria and provide "near-absolute protection" during tornadoes. To be considered a FEMA safe room, the structure must be designed and constructed to the guidelines specified in FEMA P-320, Taking Shelter from the Storm: Building a Safe Room for Your Home or Small Business (available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1454-20490-8914/fema_p_320.pdf), and FEMA P-361, Design and Construction Guidance for Community Safe Rooms (available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1508-20490-8283/fema_p_361.pdf). Consult the FEMA Webpage "Safe Rooms," at http://www.fema.gov/safe-rooms, for more information and additional resources.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Fact Sheet: Earthquakes, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1621-20490-0014/earthquakesfactsheet_final.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #2) Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Fact Sheet: Earthquakes, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1621-20490-0014/earthquakesfactsheet_final.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #3) Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank from high winds. Move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from wind and windborne debris. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #8) Explore the feasibility of procuring a backup power generator to power life safety systems in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #9) Install the necessary wiring and transfer switches to connect to a backup power generator and plan for its rapid deployment.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/tornadoes, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.
