records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures during tornado season.
records.ofc_text: Establish tornado emergency plans and procedures. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available tornado planning resources.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Conduct periodic, mandatory tornado drills for employees to practice tornado response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #13) Develop shelter-in-place plans for facility occupants if it is not safe to evacuate the facility during a natural disaster. Train employees on the plan, and conduct recurring exercises on, reviews of, and updates to the plan. Ensure contractors and frequent visitors to the facility are aware of these plans. Consult the Ready.gov Website, available at http://www.ready.gov/shelter, for more information on sheltering.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #16) Ensure all trees on facility property are far enough away to prevent them from damaging the facility if they should fall. Consult the FEMA flyer Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #17) Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #18) If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #39) Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/tornadoes, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate wildfire impacts.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of retrofitting the facility with fire-resistant materials such as tile, stucco, metal siding, brick, concrete block, and steel.
records.reference: Ready.gov, 2014, "Wildfires," April 9, http://www.ready.gov/wildfires, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Treat wood or combustible materials used in roofs, siding, decking, or trim with fire-retardant chemicals evaluated by a nationally recognized laboratory.
records.reference: Ready.gov, 2014, "Wildfires," April 9, http://www.ready.gov/wildfires, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of replacing skylights with fire-resistant-rated skylights and framing. Skylights are typically constructed with materials that can melt under intense heat.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Home Builder's Guide to Construction in Wildfire Zones: Technical Fact Sheet Series (FEMA P-737), "FS No. 10 – Windows and Skylights," September, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1652-20490-4085/fema_p_737.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Replace windows and glass doors with tempered safety glass.
records.reference: Nadel, B. A., 2004, Building Security Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of installing fire-resistant door assemblies to protect the facility from fires.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Wildfire Hazard Mitigation Handbook for Public Facilities (FEMA P-754), section D.1, p. 31-32, October, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1715-25045-2934/fema_p_754.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Protect vent openings that may allow burning embers to enter the facility.
records.reference: FEMA, 2008, Wildfire Hazard Mitigation Handbook for Public Facilities (FEMA P-754), section C.1, p. 27-29, October, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1715-25045-2934/fema_p_754.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of constructing a noncombustible retaining wall to help increase setback.
records.reference: Texas Forest Service and Institute for Business & Home Safety, undated, Protect Your Property from Wildfire: Southwest Edition, p. 17, http://texasforestservice.tamu.edu/uploadedFiles/FRP/Predictive_Services/Prepare%20Your%20Home%20for%20Wildfire%20-%20Southwest%20Edition.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Design and landscape facility grounds home with wildfire safety in mind. Select materials and plants that are fire-resistant.
records.reference: Ready.gov, 2014, "Wildfires," April 9, http://www.ready.gov/wildfires, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Relocate propane tanks at least 30 feet from the facility, and create a 10-foot noncombustible zone around them.
records.reference: Texas Forest Service and Institute for Business & Home Safety, undated, Protect Your Property from Wildfire: Southwest Edition, p.15, http://texasforestservice.tamu.edu/uploadedFiles/FRP/Predictive_Services/Prepare%20Your%20Home%20for%20Wildfire%20-%20Southwest%20Edition.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/wildfires. For more information.
records.reference: Texas Forest Service and Institute for Business & Home Safety, undated, Protect Your Property from Wildfire: Southwest Edition, p.15, http://texasforestservice.tamu.edu/uploadedFiles/FRP/Predictive_Services/Prepare%20Your%20Home%20for%20Wildfire%20-%20Southwest%20Edition.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has no plans or procedures to implement long-term mitigation measures for wildfires.
records.ofc_text: Establish specific plans and procedures to protect the facility and its critical assets from wildfires. Contact the State/local emergency management office and/or the regional FEMA office for available wildfire planning resources.
records.reference: Texas Forest Service and Institute for Business & Home Safety, undated, Protect Your Property from Wildfire: Southwest Edition, p.15, http://texasforestservice.tamu.edu/uploadedFiles/FRP/Predictive_Services/Prepare%20Your%20Home%20for%20Wildfire%20-%20Southwest%20Edition.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Conduct periodic, mandatory wildfire drills for employees to practice wildfire response procedures and condition themselves to react spontaneously and safely. Consult Prepare Your People: Wildfire Safety for Workplaces, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/1395779347101-00feb0d0db4bfdddd331a8026c09cbe8/20140318_PYP_WF_WP_508.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: America's PrepareAthon!, 2014, Prepare Your People: Wildfire Safety for Workplaces, March, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/1395779347101-00feb0d0db4bfdddd331a8026c09cbe8/20140318_PYP_WF_WP_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Establish a liaison with the local fire department or forestry office to remain alert of hazardous conditions.
records.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, Wildfire... Are you Prepared? (FEMA FA-287), August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of adding shallow rooftop pools for firefighting during wildfire season.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of installing sprinklers, water storage tanks, and automated pumps with dedicated generators to coincide with wildfire season.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Establish a maintenance plan for irrigation systems, hoses, and generators.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Establish a maintenance plan to clear all flammable vegetation (e.g., dead branches, leaves, twigs) from facility grounds during wildfire season.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Keep a 30- to 100-foot safety zone around the facility during wildfire season where facility occupants could go to survive a wildfire in the vicinity.
records.reference: FEMA/U.S. Fire Administration, 2004, Wildfire... Are you Prepared? (FEMA FA-287), August, http://www.usfa.fema.gov/downloads/pdf/publications/fa_287.pdf.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Remove all wooden objects (e.g., shingles, non-essential equipment components) from the exterior of the facility.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #51) Consult the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/wildfires. For more information.
records.reference: Nadel, B.A., 2004, Building Security: Handbook for Architectural Planning and Design, New York, NY: McGraw-Hill, ch. 12, p. 12-18.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate the impacts of severe winter storms.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of retrofitting the facility to withstand snow loads and prevent roof collapse.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 52, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Winterize the facility by insulating walls, caulking, and weather-stripping doors and windows, and installing storm windows or covering windows with plastic. Such measures can extend the life of the fuel supply.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 52, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Retrofit water pipes with enhanced insulation to prevent freezing.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 52, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Use snow fences or “living snow fences” (i.e., rows of trees or other vegetation) to limit blowing and drifting snow.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 52, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Develop plans and procedures to sand areas to improve traction, spread rock salt to melt ice, and make snow removal equipment readily available.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 52, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of installing roadway heating technology to prevent ice/snow buildup.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #17) Keep limbs on nearby trees trimmed to prevent breakage. Falling tree limbs can damage facilities, assets, and critical utility lines.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #18) If the facility does not have a backup generator, explore options to enter into a contract to have one readily available, in the event that a natural hazard disrupts power to the facility and its critical assets. Ensure that the facility is equipped (i.e., has the necessary wiring and transfer switches) to facilitate the rapid installation and operation of the generator unit.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #68) For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website, at http://www.ready.gov/winter-weather.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have deployable (i.e., non-permanent) mitigation measures to be put in place in anticipation of a severe winter storm.
records.ofc_text: Establish plans and procedures to shut off water pipes to prevent freezing and possibly bursting.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Insulate pipes with insulation or newspapers and plastic and allow faucets to drip slightly to avoid freezing.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Watch for warning signs of overstress conditions (e.g., sagging ceiling tiles, cracks in walls or masonry, and severe roof leaks) during events involving heavy snow. If conditions exist, evacuate personnel and request a structural inspection. Consult the FEMA flyer Snow Load Safety Guidance, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/1392984631969-ac57339deb6ee839a52b16b01eeee53e/FEMA_Snow_Load_508.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Risk Management Series – Snow Load Safety Guide (FEMA P-957), section 4.2, p. 4-3 to 4-4, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/7d8c55d1c4f815edf3d7e7d1c120383f/FEMA957_Snowload_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Remove snow from the roof, if possible, following proper OSHA protocol for working on rooftops to ensure safety. Heavy snow accumulation can damage a facility's roof and possibly cause it to collapse.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Risk Management Series – Snow Load Safety Guide (FEMA P-957), section 5.3, p. 5-4 to 5-6, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/7d8c55d1c4f815edf3d7e7d1c120383f/FEMA957_Snowload_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Inspect drains, gutters, downspouts, and vents for snow or ice blockage from earlier snowfalls to make room for new snow.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Risk Management Series – Snow Load Safety Guide (FEMA P-957), section 5.1, p. 5-2, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/7d8c55d1c4f815edf3d7e7d1c120383f/FEMA957_Snowload_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Risk Management Series – Snow Load Safety Guide (FEMA P-957), section 5.1, p. 5-2, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/7d8c55d1c4f815edf3d7e7d1c120383f/FEMA957_Snowload_508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate the impacts of lightning strikes.
records.ofc_text: Install lightning protection devices and methods, such as lightning rods and grounding stakes/cables, on communications infrastructure and other critical facilities.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 40, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Ensure that any installed lightning protection systems are attached properly and can withstand high winds that may accompany thunder storms. Consult the FEMA flyer Rooftop Attachment of Lightning Protection Systems in High-Wind Regions, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1605-20490-6583/fema549_apndx_e_ra7.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 40, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: Install and maintain surge protection on critical electronic equipment.
records.reference: FEMA, 2013, Mitigation Ideas: A Resource for Reducing Risks to Natural Hazards, p. 40, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1904-25045-0186/fema_mitigation_ideas_final508.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not been constructed, modified, or retrofitted to mitigate the impacts of high winds (not associated with hurricanes/tornados).
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #1) Install permanent storm shutters to protect windows to help prevent glass breakage and stop windborne objects from penetrating the facility. Always use permanent storm shutters in areas where there will be little advance warning of high winds, as permanent shutters can be closed quickly, unlike temporary plywood covers that must be retrieved from storage and mounted with bolts or screws. Consult the FEMA flyer Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #2) Explore options to retrofit roofing to secure it to the structure and increase resistance to high winds. Consult the FEMA Webpage "Protect Your Property from High Winds," at http://www.fema.gov/media-library/assets/documents/13270?id=3263, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Protect Windows and Doors with Covers, April, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-8875/how2017_how2029_protect_windows_doors_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #3) Securely anchor rooftop equipment (e.g., HVAC units, lightning protection systems, antennae) to prevent them from detaching during high winds.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.parent_question: Is the facility located in an area that experiences any of the following natural hazards?
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #38) Protect the facility's backup power generator and its fuel tank from high winds. Move the generator and tank inside or next to a building or protective structure to shield them from wind and windborne debris. Consult the FEMA flyer Install a Generator for Emergency Power, available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-0643/how2022_generator_04_11_r1.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: FEMA, 2007, Risk Management Series – Design Guide for Improving Critical Facility Safety from Flooding and High Winds (FEMA 543), section 3.3.4, p. 3-81 to 3-97, January, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1557-20490-1542/fema543_complete.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Remove Trees and Potential Windborne Missiles, April,  http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1627-20490-3107/how2020_trees_missiles_4_11.pdf, accessed June 11, 2014.

Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a perimeter fence. The lack of perimeter fencing may allow unrestricted access to the facility and critical areas within the facility.
records.ofc_text: Install fencing appropriate for the facility type. Determine the appropriate role of the fencing either to demarcate the boundary of the site to protect against trespassing; provide access control by channeling individuals through authorized access points; and/or to protect against unauthorized entry by providing increased access delay and more time for assessment.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text:
Consider the installation of fencing with the following attributes:
Type
- chain link OR
- anti-climb aluminum or steel chain link OR
- standard aluminum or steel chain link OR
- concrete OR
- brick and mortar OR
- steel OR
- wrought iron
Height
- 7 ft. high OR
- 8 ft. high OR
- 9 ft. high
Base of Fence
- anchored base
Outriggers
- outriggers OR
- outriggers angled at 45 degrees OR
- outriggers in a "Y"/"V" configuration
Enhancements
- K-rated for vehicle penetration 
- cabling to provide additional crash resistance
- coiled razor wire along the top of the fence
- coiled barbed wire along the top of the fence
- coiled razor wire along the bottom of the fence 
- coiled barbed wire along the bottom of the fence 
- spikes along the top to prevent scaling 
- privacy screening
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Explore the option of providing enhanced intrusion detection with countermeasures such as surveillance, alarms, or patrols.
records.reference: Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc, p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 91-113.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Design the fence line to maximize natural surveillance from the street to the facility and from the facility to the street, and minimize opportunities for intruders to hide.
records.reference: Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc, p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 39.

records.ofc_text:
(Ref: OFC #2) Consider the installation of fencing with the following attributes:
Type
- chain link OR
- anti-climb aluminum or steel chain link OR
- standard aluminum or steel chain link OR
- concrete OR
- brick and mortar OR
- steel OR
- wrought iron
Height
- 7 ft. high OR
- 8 ft. high OR
- 9 ft. high
Base of Fence
- anchored base
Outriggers
- outriggers OR
- outriggers angled at 45 degrees OR
- outriggers in a "Y"/"V" configuration
Enhancements
- K-rated for vehicle penetration 
- cabling to provide additional crash resistance
- coiled razor wire along the top of the fence
- coiled barbed wire along the top of the fence
- coiled razor wire along the bottom of the fence 
- coiled barbed wire along the bottom of the fence 
- spikes along the top to prevent scaling 
- privacy screening
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 206.

records.vulnerability: Fences less than 7 feet tall do little more than demarcate a boundary.
records.ofc_text: Increase the height of the fence or install outriggers with barbed wire to enhance the height and penetration delay provided by the fence, if more than boundary demarcation is appropriate. The fence should be at least 7-9 feet high. The higher the fence, the more difficult and time-consuming it is to climb.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 96.

records.vulnerability: The base of the fence at the facility is not anchored, which may allow access under the fence line.
records.ofc_text: Anchor the base of the fence to protect against access by crawling under or raising lower portion of fence. The fence should also be installed to preclude access made possible by ground erosion.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Extend the fencing fabric to within 2 inches of firm ground, and anchor the base of the fence using horizontal bottom rails, tension wires, concrete curbs, sills, sheet piling, piping, or other inexpensive materials. Horizontal bottom rails, concrete curbs, or sills can mitigate the ability to lift the fence fabric by hand (e.g., no more than 5 inches).
records.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-2, p. 15, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Bury the fence fabric (e.g., 12 inches into the ground) for additional security. This may also prevent the fencing fabric from being lifted (for example, by more than 5 inches). However, corrosion of the buried fabric must be monitored.
records.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-2, p. 15, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

records.child_node: Outriggers (e.g., barbed wire or razor wire)

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: Although the fence is equipped with outriggers, they are angled toward the facility and do not effectively deter access over the top of the fence from outside the perimeter.
records.ofc_text: Equip the fence with outriggers that are angled out and away from the facility to make climbing the fence more difficult and to prevent ladders from being placed against it. The overhang should extend far enough to increase the height of the fence by at least 1 foot. Consult UFC 4-022-03, Security Fences and Gates, available at http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, and Access Control Point Standard Drawings and Criteria, available at https://pdc.usace.army.mil/library/drawings/acp, for more information.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 274-275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: The fence is not topped with outriggers, which makes it more scalable.
records.ofc_text: Install outriggers with connecting wire, such as three strands of barbed wire at regular intervals, along the top of the fence. Outriggers typically face outward (i.e., away from the facility) and upward at approximately a 45-degree angle.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-29 to 2-30, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Install outriggers (support arms) at 45-degree angles in a single arm (towards the threat side) or “Y”/”V” configuration, constructed of a single or double outrigger consisting of 18-inch (457 mm) arm(s), each having three strands of barbed wire at regular intervals along the top of the fence. The outriggers should provide a minimum of an additional 12 inches to the fence height. Consult Access Control Point Standard Drawings and Criteria, available at https://pdc.usace.army.mil/library/drawings/acp, for more information.
records.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-9.2, p. 22-24, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: The fence is topped with outriggers (support arms), but connecting wire (e.g., barbed or razor wire) is not installed on the supporting arms. Without connecting wire, the outriggers serve only as extensions of the fence posts.
records.ofc_text: Install connecting wire, such as three strands of barbed wire, at regular intervals along the top of the fence.
records.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-9.2, p. 22-24, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

records.vulnerability: The fence lacks privacy screening to obscure the observation of onsite facilities, critical  equipment, etc.
records.ofc_text: Add slats or mesh fabric to the fence to provide screening, if privacy is appropriate. Such screening can provide opportunities for cover and concealment and preclude visual assessment by security force. However, these techniques should not be used if air flow through the fence is important.
records.reference: McDonald, J.D., 2012, Electric Power Substations Engineering, 3rd Edition, Boca Raton, FL: CRC Press, p. 16-13.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: Although the fence provides a clear boundary marker, identifies restricted areas, and penetration delay, additional enhancements can provide additional penetration delay of the fence line (e.g., traversal, vehicle ramming) and more time for assessment.
records.ofc_text: Install enhancements to the fence to improve its effectiveness and penetration delay.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Eliminate the top rail of a fence to make it more difficult to grasp.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 271, p. 275; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 263.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Integrate other security systems with the fence, including sensors, cameras, or other intrusion detection devices, as appropriate.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 339-353.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of integrating chain-link fabric into perimeter fencing which covers the chain-link fencing to prevent penetration and provide concealment.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 270-271.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Apply welded wire-mesh fabric to the fence. Welded wire-mesh fabric is welded tightly together to prevent toe or finger hold and adds greater resistance against cutting.
records.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-4, p. 19, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of adding barbed, taped concertina to the top of the fence. Taped concertina can significantly decrease the ability to scale a fence.
records.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-4, p. 19, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of installing "tangle-foot wire." Tangle-foot wire is barbed wire strung across two posts no higher than 12 inches. It is used outside a perimeter fence or between double fences to provide extra protection from an intruder. Liability issues should be considered when installing tangle-foot wire.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-29, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of adding tunneling preventive measures for gaps that would allow for entry under the fence.
records.reference: DOD, 2013, Unified Facilities Criteria (UFC) - Security Fences and Gates (UFC 4-022-03), section 2-12.8, p. 34-37, October, http://www.wbdg.org/ccb/DOD/UFC/ufc_4_022_03.pdf, accessed July 10, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: The fence lacks a clear zone. A perimeter without a clear zone may aid intruders in gaining undetected access to a facility by providing areas for cover and concealment.
records.ofc_text: Establish clear zones on both sides of the fence to provide an unobstructed view to enhance detection and assessment. The dimensions of clears zones may vary depending on the asset being protected and level of protection required. Provide as much open space as possible between the fence and the facility.
records.reference: FEMA, 2003, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426), section 2.4.1, p. 2-29, December, http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1455-20490-6222/fema426.pdf, accessed May 20, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Ensure clear zones are free of debris, vegetation, and any items that could provide cover and concealment. When it is impossible to have adequate clear zones because of property lines or natural or manmade features, it may be necessary to increase the height of the perimeter barrier, increase security-patrol coverage, and/or add more security lighting along that portion of the perimeter. Failure to establish and maintain a clear zone along the fence minimizes its effectiveness. Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 272; Fischer, R.J., E.P. Halibozek, and D.C. Walters, 2013, Introduction to Security, 9th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 229-239; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 39, p. 91-113, p. 259.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Ensure that for high security applications a clear zone of 20 feet or more separates a perimeter barrier from exterior structures, parking areas, and natural features. A clear zone of 50 feet or more should separate a perimeter barrier from structures within the protected area, except when a building wall constitutes part of a perimeter barrier.Consult Site and Urban Design for Security: Guidance against Potential Terrorist Attacks (FEMA 430), available at http://www.fema.gov/media-library-data/20130726-1624-20490-9648/fema430.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: Muuss, J.P., and D. Rabern, 2006, The Complete Guide for CPP Examination Preparation, Boca Raton, FL: Auerbach Publications, p. 174.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Implement a grounds maintenance program to check and clear vegetation regularly from the fence's clear zone. Vegetation should not exceed 8 inches in height. Also, because fallen trees can damage fences, management of tall trees in the vicinity of the fence is important, especially in areas where a fence passes through a remote area.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
records.child_node: Is the area on either side of the fence free of objects / structures that would aid in traversing the fence (trees, sheds, barrels, etc.)?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: There are objects and/or structures adjacent to the fence that may aid traversing the fence.
records.ofc_text: Remove all objects near the fence that would aid traversing it. Keep a clear zone free of any item that could aid an intruder in breeching or circumventing the perimeter fence.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 272.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Ensure security force roving patrols include surveillance of the perimeter fence and objects/structures that remain near the fence line.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
records.child_node: Fence is clearly marked with visible, well-placed "warning" signs?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.vulnerability: The fence is not clearly marked or identified with warning signs.
records.ofc_text: Install warning signs that are easy to understand along the fence. In areas where two or more languages are commonly spoken, the warning signs must use both (or more) languages. Post signage at intervals of no more than 100 feet.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.4, p. 2-70, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
records.ofc_text: Post visible, well-placed warning signs on the fence. Signs may act as a deterrent and/or provide safety information for unauthorized personnel. Signs are usually placed on boundary fences, typically at 50-foot intervals, to indicate ownership and to warn of possible danger within. In areas where two or more languages are commonly spoken, the warning signs must use both (or more) languages.
records.reference: FEMA, 2011, Risk Management Series – Reference Manual to Mitigate Potential Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings (FEMA 426/BIPS 06), section 2.4.4, p. 2-70, October, http://www.dhs.gov/xlibrary/assets/st/st-bips-06.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have fencing?
Does the facility have fencing?
Do Gates Exist?
records.vulnerability: There are no gates at openings in the fence line. The lack of gates provides individuals and vehicles with unrestricted access to the facility.
records.ofc_text: Install gates, providing appropriate levels of access control and/or penetration delay against individuals, at identified gaps in perimeter fencing. Install barriers/systems at gates to provide vehicle access control and/or vehicle penetration delay, as appropriate.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 112-113, p. 270; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 47, p. 180, p. 263-265.

records.parent_question: Do Gates Exist?
records.ofc_text: Install vehicle gates for restricted areas so that they are set back from the roadway to ensure that temporary delays caused by identification checks will not cause traffic hazards. Provide sufficient space to allow for spot checks, inspections, and searches of vehicles without impeding the flow of traffic.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 112-113, p. 270; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2012, Protection of Assets: Physical Security, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 264-265.

records.parent_question: Do Gates Exist?
records.ofc_text: Integrate other security systems, including sensors, cameras, or other intrusion detection devices, at gates, as appropriate.
records.reference: Fennelly, L., 2013, Effective Physical Security, 4th Edition, Waltham, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 339-353.

records.vulnerability: Moveable bollards may be an option to slow traffic, but they would be unable to completely stop a vehicle traveling at a high rate of speed unless they were hydraulically operated.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing non-moveable bollards or other non-moveable barriers. Stationary bollards or barriers can significantly decrease the chance of a vehicle penetrating a facility.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2006, Vulnerability Assessment of Physical Protection Systems, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 212.

Resilience Operations
records.child_node: Is there a manager/department in charge of business continuity?

records.parent_question: Resilience Operations
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a designated business continuity manager. The lack of a designated business continuity manager may hinder the implementation of business continuity policies, programs, or procedures.
records.ofc_text: Appoint or nominate a person with appropriate seniority and authority to be accountable for business continuity management policy and implementation. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/program-management.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.2, p. 13.

Does the facility participate in any emergency preparedness working groups?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not participate in emergency preparedness working groups.
records.ofc_text: Join relevant emergency preparedness working groups. Establish liaisons and regular communications with Federal, State, and local-level emergency preparedness working groups, as well as private sector/industry working groups, to share expertise and prepare to better respond to emergencies.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5.2, p. 13.

Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a written business continuity plan.
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
records.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18, section 8.5, p. 19.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should contain the following elements: a) defined roles and responsibilities for personnel with authority during and following an incident; b) a process for activating the response; c) details to manage the immediate consequences of a disruptive incident (with regard to the welfare of individuals; strategic, tactical, and operational response options, and prevention of further loss or unavailability of prioritized activities); d) details on how and under what circumstances the facility will communicate with employees and their relatives, key interested parties, and emergency contacts; e) how the facility will continue or recover its prioritized activities within predetermined timeframes; f) details of the facility's media response following an incident; and g) a process for standing down once the incident is over. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
records.reference: ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.4.4, p. 18, section 8.5, p. 19.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive business continuity plan to be implemented in the aftermath of an incident. The business continuity plan should identify stakeholders that need to be notified; critical and time-sensitive applications; alternative work sites; vital records, contact lists, processes, and functions that must be maintained; and personnel, procedures, and resources that are necessary while the facility recovers. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/continuity.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.9, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.ofc_text: For government agencies, a continuity of operations plan may be preferred to a business continuity plan. Develop a continuity of operations plan to document the overarching strategy, policies, and procedures required to support the organization's continuity of operations program. Visit the FEMA Website for related resources, at http://www.fema.gov/planning-templates.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.9, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.ofc_text: Refer to the Business Continuity Planning Suite, developed by DHS’ National Protection and Programs Directorate and FEMA, for assistance with business continuity planning. This tool is scalable for use by organizations of any size and consists of a business continuity plan training; automated business continuity plan and disaster recovery plan generators; and a self-directed exercise for testing an implemented business continuity plan. The Business Continuity Planning Suite is available at http://www.ready.gov/business-continuity-planning-suite.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.9, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.child_node: Has the plan been approved by senior management?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.vulnerability: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's business continuity plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of business continuity concerns.
records.ofc_text: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the business continuity plan. The successful establishment of business continuity management is dependent upon its integration with strategic and day-to-day facility management and its alignment with business priorities. To be effective, business continuity management must be recognized from the outset by senior management. Business continuity management must always start at the top – for the essential reason that business continuity management is about what’s important and time-sensitive.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 5, p. 13, section 6.7, p. 20; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 5.2, p.10.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not exercise its business continuity plan annually.
records.ofc_text: Exercise and test the business continuity plan annually to ensure procedures are consistent with business continuity objectives. Use appropriate scenarios, and involve relevant interested parties. Produce post-exercise reports containing outcomes, recommendations, and actions to implement improvements.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 9.3.4, p. 37; ISO, 2012, Societal Security – Business Continuity Management Systems – Requirements (ISO 22301), section 8.5, p. 19.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written business continuity plan?
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the business continuity plan through annual exercises. Ensure exercises are designed to accomplish the following: 1) Identify planning and procedural deficiencies; 2) Test or validate recently changed procedures or plans; 3) Clarify roles and responsibilities; 4) Obtain participant feedback and recommendations for program improvement; 5) Measure improvement compared to performance objectives; 6) Improve coordination between internal and external teams, organizations, and entities; 7) Validate training and education; and 8) Identify additional resources and assess the capabilities of existing resources, including personnel and equipment needed for effective business continuity and recovery.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

Is there an alternate site for continuity of business?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have an alternate site for continuity of business when the primary location is not accessible.
records.ofc_text: Establish an alternate site to continue business when the primary location is not accessible. Ensure there is sufficient distance between the alternate site and the facility so they are not likely to be affected by the same incident. Identify the location of the alternate site in the business continuity plan and define who should report to the site and under what conditions.
records.reference: BSI, 2006, Business Continuity Management – Part 1: Code of Practice (BS 25999-1:2006), section 7.4, p. 22.

Is there a manager/department in charge of emergency management?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a designated emergency manager. The lack of a designated emergency manager may hinder the implementation of emergency management policies, programs, or procedures.
records.ofc_text: Designate an employee as emergency manager to develop, implement, and coordinate all emergency management-related activities.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 4.2, p. 1600-6, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 16.

Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a written emergency operation/emergency action plan.
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive emergency action plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 5.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive emergency operation/emergency action plan specific to the facility. The emergency operation/action plan should assign responsibilities for carrying out specific actions to protect people (including those with special needs), property, operations, and the environment in an emergency, and to provide incident stabilization. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.8, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?

records.vulnerability: The corporate-level emergency operation/emergency action plan does not reflect facility-specific needs.
records.ofc_text: Develop a facility-specific addendum to the corporate emergency operation/emergency action plan. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/emergency, and consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.8, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.8, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.vulnerability: Senior management personnel have not approved the facility's emergency operation/emergency action plan, which suggests a possible lack of buy-in or oversight of emergency concerns.
records.ofc_text: Acquire the signature and approval of a senior manager or designated representative for the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 11.

records.child_node: Has the plan been coordinated with emergency responders?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.vulnerability: The facility has not coordinated the emergency operation/emergency action plan with emergency responders.
records.ofc_text: Coordinate the emergency operation/emergency action plan with first responders. Establish MOUs/MOAs as necessary.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Share critical information about the facility (e.g., floor plans, location of emergency equipment, notification and contact lists) with emergency responders. Consult Developing and Maintaining Emergency Operations Plans, available at http://www.fema.gov/pdf/about/divisions/npd/CPG_101_V2.pdf, for more information.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.child_node: Is it reviewed annually with emergency responders?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders.
records.ofc_text: Review the emergency operation/emergency action plan annually with emergency responders, to ensure these agencies are familiar with the facility and its emergency response plans, policies, and procedures.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Develop and implement procedures to review the emergency operation/emergency action plan, especially any revisions that have been implemented within the past year, with emergency responders on an annual basis.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Provide a copy of the emergency operation/emergency action to the appropriate emergency response agencies.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 23.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not train personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan.
records.ofc_text: Train all personnel on the emergency operation/emergency action plan at least annually. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.0, p. 19, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 7, p. 1600-9 to 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Train all personnel on emergency procedures for life safety (e.g., evacuation, shelter, shelter-in-place and lockdown). Train members of the emergency response team so they are familiar with their role and responsibilities as defined within the emergency operation/emergency action plan. Provide advanced training to team leaders, including incident command system training, so they can effectively lead their teams. Maintain records documenting the scope of training, participants, instructors, and duration.
records.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Provide training to obtain and maintain certifications to those emergency response team members that administer First Aid or CPR, or use AEDs.
records.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Provide training in accordance with the applicable OSHA regulation for those employees who use portable fire extinguishers, fire hoses, or other firefighting equipment.
records.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Provide appropriate training to employees who respond to HAZMAT spills.
records.reference: Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.ofc_text: Conduct regular drills and exercises to validate the emergency operation/emergency action plan and to evaluate the ability of personnel to carry out their assigned roles and responsibilities. For more information, visit the Ready.gov Website at http://www.ready.gov/business/testing/exercises.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Review protective actions for life safety and conduct evacuation drills. Conduct sheltering and lockdown drills.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Ready.gov, 2012, "Training," November 1, http://www.ready.gov/business/implementation/training, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Evaluate the emergency operation/emergency action plan through annual exercises. Ensure exercises are designed to accomplish the following: 1) Identify planning and procedural deficiencies; 2) Test or validate recently changed procedures or plans; 3) Clarify roles and responsibilities; 4) Obtain participant feedback and recommendations for program improvement; 5) Measure improvement compared to performance objectives; 6) Improve coordination between internal and external teams, organizations, and entities; 7) Validate training and education; 8) Increase awareness and understanding of hazards and the potential impacts of hazards on the facility; and 9) Identify additional resources and assess the capabilities of existing resources, including personnel and equipment needed for effective response.
records.reference: NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), ch. 8, p. 1600-10, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Document all exercise results. Critically evaluate exercises to assess whether goals and objectives were achieved, the effectiveness of participation, and whether the emergency response management system will function as anticipated in the case of a real crisis. Modify the emergency operation/emergency action plan as necessary.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.5.3, p. 37, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written Emergency Operation/ Emergency Action plan?
records.ofc_text: Consult the Homeland Security Exercise and Evaluation Program Website, at https://www.llis.dhs.gov/HSEEP, for a set of fundamental principles that frame a common approach to exercises, among other helpful resources.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.5.3, p. 37, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

Does the facility exchange information with a local or state Emergency Operation Center?

Does the facility have an Incident Management and Command Center (IMCC)?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have an IMCC. Without an IMCC, the facility lacks a dedicated area to direct and oversee emergency management activities.
records.ofc_text: Establish an IMCC dedicated to facilitating the coordination and support of emergency management activities.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014; NPFA, 2013, Standard on Disaster/Emergency Management and Business Continuity Programs (NPFA 1600), section 6.7.1.1, p. 1600-9, http://www.nfpa.org/assets/files/AboutTheCodes/1600/1600-13-PDF.pdf, accessed June 17, 2014; Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International, p. 20.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have an Incident Management and Command Center (IMCC)?
records.ofc_text: Implement access control measures for the IMCC. Provide members of all teams who staff the IMCC with 24/7 access.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have an Incident Management and Command Center (IMCC)?
records.ofc_text: Ensure the IMCC has an uninterruptible power supply; essential computer, telecommunications, heating/ventilating/air conditioning systems; and other support systems to continue operations for at least 72 hours. In addition, identify and store emergency supplies in the IMCC.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2009, Organizational Resilience: Security, Preparedness, and Continuity Management Systems – Requirements with Guidance for Use (ASIS SPC.1-2009), section A.4.7, p. 33, http://www.ndsu.edu/fileadmin/emgt/ASIS_SPC.1-2009_Item_No._1842.pdf, accessed June 16, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have an Incident Management and Command Center (IMCC)?
records.ofc_text: Determine the feasibility of including the following elements as part of the IMCC: sleeping quarters, dining/food preparation space, portable restrooms, briefing areas, communication area, and proper equipment and backup. Include these elements as appropriate. Ensure there is adequate parking.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have an Incident Management and Command Center (IMCC)?
records.vulnerability: The facility's IMCC is located onsite. An event that impacts the facility may also make the IMCC unavailable for crisis management.
records.ofc_text: Relocate the IMCC to an offsite location to ensure emergency management facilities are available and not impacted by an event at the facility.

records.ofc_text: Activate the IMCC annually, and complete an after-action report following the activation.

Does the facility security plan utilize different threat levels?
records.vulnerability: The facility security plan does not utilize a threat level system to guide the implementation of increased protective measures as threat levels increase.
records.ofc_text: Incorporate a threat level system into the security plan.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, Appendix C, p. C-1 to C-4, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility security plan utilize different threat levels?
records.ofc_text: Incorporate the DHS NTAS into the security plan. The NTAS provides information timely, detailed information about terrorist threats to the public, government agencies, first responders, airports and other transportation hubs, and the private sector. Consult the DHS NTAS Website, at http://www.dhs.gov/national-terrorism-advisory-system, for more information.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, Appendix C, p. C-1 to C-4, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility security plan utilize different threat levels?
records.ofc_text: Incorporate MARSEC levels into the security plan. MARSEC is a three-tiered system employed by the USGS to communicate with maritime industry partners of potential pre-planned responses to threats. MARSEC levels adjust in conjunction with NTAS alerts. Consult the USGS MARSEC Levels Website, at http://www.uscg.mil/safetylevels/whatismarsec.asp, for more information.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, Appendix C, p. C-1 to C-4, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility security plan utilize different threat levels?
records.ofc_text: Incorporate an industry threat level system into the security plan.
