records.child_node: Are different protective measures employed/implemented during elevated threat situations?

records.parent_question: Does the facility security plan utilize different threat levels?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not implement additional protective measures during elevated threats situations, which may leave the facility open to attack and significant damage.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of employing and implementing additional protective measures to increase the overall security posture of the facility as threat levels increase. Options may include additional access controls; barriers; increased communications and notification; enhanced cyber security; additional infrastructure upgrades and redundancy; enhanced incident response; additional monitoring and surveillance detection; and planning and preparedness.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, Appendix C, p. C-1 to C-4, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility security plan utilize different threat levels?
records.ofc_text: Conduct planning and coordination and gather resources to implement additional protective measures appropriate for the facility during elevated threat situations in a timely manner. Train facility personnel on their responsibilities associated with these protective measures, and exercise these measures annually to evaluate facility preparedness and program effectiveness.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, Appendix C, p. C-1 to C-4, http://www.fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

Overall Security Activity History and  Background Comments
Does the facility have a security force?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a security force. The lack of an adequately sized, well-trained, and well-equipped security force limits a facility's ability to respond to security incidents and/or emergencies.
records.ofc_text: Explore the feasibility of establishing or contracting a security force to actively protect assets, property, and employees, and to rapidly respond to security incidents and/or emergencies. Depending on State and local regulations, security personnel may be subject to registration, licensing, certification, specific training, and other requirements.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-7, p. 9-5 to 9-7, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a security force?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a surge capacity plan to provide additional security personnel during special circumstances or elevated threat.
records.ofc_text: Establish a surge capacity plan to augment the security force during special circumstances, for example, during special events or natural disasters, and elevated threat situations. Options may include law enforcement officers (MOA, contract, and/or off-duty) or contract security (as part of an existing contract and/or another contract). Detail in the surge capacity plan the roles, responsibilities, and chain of command for both regular and surge forces.
records.reference: Fischer, R.J., and G. Green, 2004, Introduction to Security, 7th Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 48-53.

records.vulnerability: (Ref: V #3) The facility does not have a surge capacity plan to provide additional security personnel during special circumstances or elevated threat.
records.ofc_text: (Ref: OFC #7) Establish a surge capacity plan to augment the security force during special circumstances, for example, during special events or natural disasters, and elevated threat situations. Options may include law enforcement officers (MOA, contract, and/or off-duty) or contract security (as part of an existing contract and/or another contract). Detail in the surge capacity plan the roles, responsibilities, and chain of command for both regular and surge forces.
records.reference: Fischer, R.J., and G. Green, 2004, Introduction to Security, 7th Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc., p. 48-53.

records.vulnerability: The security force does not conduct roving patrols that cover the facility's SAAs or other areas where monitoring and protection is necessary. If performed, roving patrols would allow the security force to more effectively monitor critical assets and areas, and deter, report, and respond to incidents.
records.ofc_text: Ensure that SAAs and other areas as necessary are covered by roving patrols conducted by the security force. Routes and times for patrols should also be varied at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a security force?
records.vulnerability: The security force does not conduct random roving patrols. Security patrols that are conducted in a regular, unvarying sequence may be observed and exploited.
records.ofc_text: Vary routes and times of roving patrols at frequent intervals to preclude establishing a routine that may be observed by potential intruders.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-5, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.child_node: Does the security force receive training?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a security force?
records.vulnerability: The security force does not receive any type of training. A robust security force training program can decrease mistakes, increase proficiency, and enhance a facility's overall security posture.
records.ofc_text: Develop or contract a formal training program for the security force that covers topics such as emergency response, SOPs (for example, facility-specific SOPs, ICS/NIMS), screening and access, and, if appropriate, weapons and self-defense. Continuation/in-service training should be provided to maintain and enhance job proficiency. Document training and retain training records.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a security force?
records.ofc_text: Employ security professionals certified or licensed through a Federal or State program. Ensure the training requirements they fulfilled to become certified/licensed will equip them to fulfill the facility's security requirements.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 9-6, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.vulnerability: The facility does not have comprehensive post orders for the security force, which may result in situations where security personnel do not fulfill their duties and/or perform outside the scope of their duties.
records.ofc_text: Develop comprehensive post orders and make them available at each guard station.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-8, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a security force?
records.ofc_text: Develop post orders that describe in detail the duties and responsibilities of the security force. After comprehensive post orders are developed, ensure that they are kept current and accessible. Comprehensive post orders may serve to express the policies of the protected enterprise, summarize required officer duties, avoid problems associated with of word-of-mouth instructions, and provide a basis for site-specific training. They are the vital link between the requirements of the facility and the ability of security personnel to effectively meet those requirements.
records.reference: Department of the Army, 2010, ATTP 3-39.32 (FM 3-19.30) – Physical Security, p. 3-8, http://fas.org/irp/doddir/army/attp3-39-32.pdf, accessed June 19, 2014.

records.child_node: Is there a dedicated command and control or operation center for the guard force?

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a security force?
records.vulnerability: The security force does not have a dedicated command and control/operation center. Without a dedicated security center, the security force lacks a control point for coordination, monitoring and dispatch, and other critical activities.

records.parent_question: Security Department
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a designated security manager. The lack of a security manager may hinder the application of security policies, programs, or procedures.
records.ofc_text: Designate an employee to act as a security manager and task that person with developing, implementing, and coordinating all security-related activities. If possible, choose an employee with previous security experience. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.

Does the facility have a written security plan?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not have a written security plan.
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year. Consult The Critical Success Factor Method: Establishing a Foundation for Enterprise Security Management, available at http://www.ready.gov/document/critical-success-factor-method-establishing-foundation-enterprise-security-management-0, for more information.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written security plan?
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address issues such as the following: protection of employees, contractors/vendors, visitors, and executives; protection of sensitive information; protection of funds; facility access control procedures; suspicious activity reporting procedures; employee termination procedures; parking; mail security; background checks; prohibited items; security force; end-of-day security checks; control and accountability of equipment (including keys); electronic security systems (including CCTV); physical security inspection programs; and employee security awareness training programs. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written security plan?
records.ofc_text: Develop a comprehensive security plan specific to the facility. The plan should address topics such as the following: physical security measures and systems, security force, access control procedures, information protection, and security awareness training. In addition, the plan should include elements such as the following: an assessment of possible security risks; a review of threats to the facility and the facility's vulnerabilities; an up-to-date point of contact roster for key personnel responsible for security and first responders; and identification of critical assets or areas. Train personnel on the plan, and exercise the plan at least once a year.
records.reference: Garcia, M.L., 2008, The Design and Evaluation of Physical Protection Systems, 2nd Edition, Burlington, MA: Elsevier Inc.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have a written security plan?

records.ofc_text: Develop procedures for handling suspicious packages.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Crisis Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have procedures for suspicious packages?
records.ofc_text: Incorporate procedures on how to handle suspicious packages into initial and annual security training programs.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have procedures for suspicious packages?
records.ofc_text: Implement U.S. Postal Service mail center security procedures to assess, prevent, and respond to threats such as suspicious letters and packages; bomb threats; and chemical, biological, or radiological contamination.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have procedures for suspicious packages?
records.ofc_text: Prominently display informational materials on suspicious package indicators in the mailroom.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have procedures for suspicious packages?
records.ofc_text: Develop a protocol to prevent air contamination (e.g., turn off fans or ventilation units, shut down the air handling system in the facility). Refer to the Ready.gov Website for more information about biological and chemical threats, at http://www.ready.gov/biological-threats, and http://www.ready.gov/chemical-threats.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Does the facility have procedures for suspicious packages?
records.ofc_text: Refer to the Ready.gov Website for information about suspicious packages and letters, at http://www.ready.gov/explosions.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

Characterize security information communication
records.child_node: Does the facility communicate security information to personnel?

records.parent_question: Characterize security information communication
records.vulnerability: The facility does not communicate security information to personnel.
records.ofc_text: Develop a communication plan that covers voice and data transfer of security-related information to all personnel.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Characterize security information communication
records.ofc_text: Develop a process for communicating the current security situation and reminders of appropriate response procedures to all personnel. Include procedures for communicating with personnel who are not on site during an incident.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Characterize security information communication
records.ofc_text: Enhance security awareness through the distribution of security-related newsletter and emails, and the display of security-related information (e.g., posters) that address potential threats, training opportunities, initiatives, and concerns.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Characterize security information communication
records.ofc_text: Regularly test notification protocols through drills and exercises.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Security Management, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

Are background checks conducted?
records.vulnerability: The facility does not conduct any background checks.
records.ofc_text: Conduct background checks on all employees.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Are background checks conducted?
records.ofc_text: Conduct initial and recurring background checks on all employees.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Are background checks conducted?
records.ofc_text: Conduct initial and recurring background checks on all employees working in positions of trust or who are assigned to critical or sensitive areas.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Are background checks conducted?
records.ofc_text: Require contracting companies, temporary employment agencies, and/or vendors to conduct background checks on their personnel who will work at and/or visit the facility and to make such records available for audit.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Are background checks conducted?
records.vulnerability: Although the facility conducts background checks, the type of checks conducted provides minimal information, creating a false sense of security in the workplace.
records.ofc_text: Explore options to conduct more comprehensive background checks to gather detailed information about personnel, especially those working in positions of trust or who are assigned to critical or sensitive areas.
records.reference: Walsh, T.J., and R.J. Healy, 2011, Protection of Assets: Investigation, M. Knoke, Ed., Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Are background checks conducted?
records.ofc_text: Conduct national criminal history background checks to thoroughly investigate personnel. Review credit reports especially for personnel in financial management positions where there is potential for fraud and embezzlement.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2014, Auditing Management Systems: Risk, Resilience, Security, and Continuity—Guidance for Application (ANSI/ASIS SPC.2-2014), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Are background checks conducted?
records.ofc_text: Seek appropriate consultation, for example, with your legal department, for guidance on conducting comprehensive background checks.
records.reference: ASIS International, 2014, Auditing Management Systems: Risk, Resilience, Security, and Continuity—Guidance for Application (ANSI/ASIS SPC.2-2014), Alexandria, VA: ASIS International.

records.parent_question: Are background checks conducted?
records.ofc_text: Conduct recurring background checks on employees as appropriate. records.ofc_text: Secure all sensitive information as appropriate (e.g., locked file cabinets, locked room).
